hero.name: Alejandro Piad Morffis
hero.bootComment: # apiad.net · v4.2 — made with ai in havana, cuba
hero.whoami: alejandro piad morffis — computer scientist, educator, neurosymbolic-ai researcher.
hero.unameHtml: apiad 5.18.0-havana-cu #1 SMP PhD CS · tenured · univ. of havana
hero.taglineHtml: Tenured Professor of Computer Science, Univ. of Havana · Neurosymbolic AI researcher · Co-founder Syalia · Director GIA-UH · Writes The Computist Journal.

paragraphs: I'm a computer scientist and educator living in Havana, Cuba. I teach Programming, Compilers and AI at the University of Havana, and I research neurosymbolic AI — the messy, beautiful frontier where LLMs meet classical symbolic methods.

I co-founded Syalia, where we build pragmatic AI software, and I direct GIA-UH, a research group on democratizing machine learning. I write The Computist Journal — 6,000+ readers, mostly about how computer science actually works underneath the hype.

items.headlineHtml: Writing a book on neurosymbolic methods.
items.bodyHtml: Draft one is at ~60%; trying to keep it shorter than my PhD thesis.
A multi-modal embedded DB and a Python context-engineering toolkit — both open source.

items.headlineHtml: Teaching a graduate course on LLM reasoning.
items.bodyHtml: UH, spring '26. Syllabus draws heavily from neurosymbolic + classical AI.
Onboarding three new master's students working on Spanish-language NLP for low-resource settings.

items.headlineHtml: Reading: The Society of Mind + recent CoT-reasoning papers.
items.bodyHtml: Looking for the seams where symbolic structure helps LLMs think, not just talk.

posts.title: Why neurosymbolic AI is having its moment, again

posts.subtitle: A lab-first curriculum from four years at UH.

posts.title: A working definition of reasoning
posts.subtitle: Draft notes I keep returning to.

posts.title: Context engineering isn't prompt engineering
posts.subtitle: Why I built a whole library around it.

posts.title: Small models, sharp tools
posts.subtitle: Why I keep coming back to 1-3B parameter models for teaching.

library.descriptionHtml: Every chapter ships as a blog post first, then folds into one of four books in progress. The reader at books.apiad.net is always free — no account, no paywall.

books.title: The Science of Computation
books.desc: A grand view of computer science — foundations, systems, software engineering, AI. Zero formulas or code.

books.title: Mostly Harmless AI
books.desc: Essays on AI and its impact on society — software, education, existential risks, limits, futures.

books.title: How to Train your Chatbot
books.desc: Hands-on guide to building LLM applications — prompts, RAG, tools, agents, and how LLMs actually work.

books.title: Mostly Harmless Graphs
books.desc: Graphs from theory to algorithms to applications. Each chapter opens with a concrete problem.

backburner: How to Think Like a Computer Scientist

research.introHtml: My PhD was on knowledge discovery for natural language. These days I work on neurosymbolic architectures — getting LLMs to cooperate with structured knowledge, symbolic solvers, and classical AI. Find the papers on:
